name: ConvertKit (Kit)
what_it_does: Email marketing platform built for creators and online businesses, with subscriber tagging, automation sequences, and a REST API for managing subscribers, forms, sequences, and broadcasts programmatically.
use_cases: Adding and tagging subscribers based on application events or purchase actions
Triggering email automation sequences when users complete key milestones
Syncing subscriber data and custom fields from application databases
Pulling subscriber engagement metrics for creator analytics dashboards
Managing opt-in forms and landing page subscriber capture programmatically
not_for: Transactional email (use Postmark, Resend, or SendGrid for receipts and notifications)
High-volume enterprise marketing automation (use Marketo or HubSpot)
Non-creator business use cases — platform is optimized for individual creators
best_when: You're a creator, course seller, or small online business and want to automate subscriber management and email sequences via a clean API.
avoid_when: You need complex enterprise marketing automation, transactional email, or multi-channel campaigns beyond email newsletters.

auth.notes: v3 API uses API key (account-scoped). v4 API uses OAuth 2.0 with scopes. Both versions are currently supported. Use v4 OAuth for new integrations — it's more secure and scoped.

pricing.paid_tiers: Creator: from $25/month; Creator Pro: from $50/month — scales with subscriber count
pricing.notes: Very generous free tier at 10,000 subscribers. API access is available on all plans including free.

performance.rate_limits: 120 requests per minute per API key

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API returns standard HTTP codes with JSON error details. Subscriber duplicate handling returns descriptive messages.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Subscriber creation/update via email is idempotent — updating existing subscriber doesn't create duplicate. Tag operations are idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: v3 and v4 API have different schemas — carefully select which version to use and don't mix
Subscriber state (active, inactive, bounced) affects deliverability — agents should check state before actions
Tags vs segments are different concepts — tags are applied to subscribers, sequences have their own enrollment logic
Rate limit of 120 req/min is moderate — bulk subscriber operations need batching
Webhook verification uses a secret shared at webhook creation — validate signatures to prevent spoofing
